div.p: Trade-offs: Safety vs Precision vs Implementation Complexity

g.text: Preemption precision | Any instruction | Safe points only | Safe points only
Time-slice guarantee | ✓ Exact | ✗ Depends on checks | ✓ Approximate
Compiler support | ✗ None needed | ✓ Required | ⚠ Optional
FFI compatibility | ✓ Works | ✗ Cannot yield in FFI | ⚠ Limited
Signal safety | Critical | N/A | Minimal (just set flag)
Latency bound | ~0 | Check frequency | Check frequency
Used by | Early Go (abandoned) | Modern Go, Tokio | This runtime

div.p: Why Hybrid? The hybrid approach balances safety and fairness:

ol.li: Timer provides fairness guarantee — no coroutine runs >10ms unchecked
Safe-point checks prevent corruption — never interrupt mid-operation
Signal handler is trivial — just set a flag (async-signal-safe)
Graceful degradation — uncooperative code still makes progress (slower) Implementation Tips:

ul.li: Insert cooperative_yield() at loop boundaries, before I/O, after function calls
Use 10ms time slices (balance fairness vs overhead)
In debug builds, warn if coroutine runs >50ms without yielding

text.tspan: // Kernel delivers signal at time slice
// Signal handler runs on current stack
// DANGER: Very few functions are safe here!
// Kernel delivers signal at time slice
// Signal handler runs on current stack
// DANGER: Very few functions are safe here!

div.p: • Preempts at any instruction • No compiler cooperation needed • Precise time slicing (10ms guaranteed) • Works with unmodified binaries
• Async-signal-safe only (no malloc, printf) • Can interrupt mid-operation • Stack handling is tricky • Complex interaction with syscalls • Risk: corruption, deadlock
• Only preempts at safe points • No signal handling complexity • Predictable overhead (constant-time) • No risk of mid-operation interrupt
• Requires compiler support • Doesn't work with FFI/libraries • Long loops without calls can starve • Latency depends on check frequency

text.tspan: // Called in loops, before I/O, etc.
// Called in loops, before I/O, etc.

div.p: • Timer precision for fairness • Only yields at safe points • No signal handler complexity • Works without full compiler support • Best of both worlds
• Still requires some cooperation • Latency bounded by safe-point freq • Manual insertion without compiler • Bad code can still starve
